pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Automate drafting, summarization, and artifact linking as safe defaults, provided outputs are clearly marked as AI-generated/drafts so a human can inspect before acting.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 explicitly approves automatic drafting, summarization, and artifact linking when clearly marked as generated and inspectable by a human. S5 lists drafting and artifact linking as safe defaults. S4 supports internal draft summaries as advisory when not confused with final commitments.

claimJudgments.claimText: Require human approval for any action that changes obligations or operations: policy changes, customer commitments, release blocking, external/customer-facing messages; record the approver in the artifact trail.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 rejects automatic policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking because they change obligations or stop work without accountable approval. S5 says release blocks, customer commitments, policy changes, and external messages require an approver recorded in the artifact trail. S4 supports human review for customer-facing commitments.

claimJudgments.claimText: Introduce and enforce explicit status labels (e.g., draft, recommendation, approved action, blocked) because a prior automation caused teams to treat a recommendation as an approved decision and pause rollout work unnecessarily.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 directly describes the incident where a recommendation was mistaken for an approved decision, causing two teams to pause rollout work, and asks for explicit labels: draft, recommendation, approved action, and blocked. S5 reinforces this taxonomy.

claimJudgments.claimText: UI should separate evidence, recommendation, and action controls, and avoid placing an execution button directly beside an AI recommendation; this layout correlates with higher user acceptance and reduces cancellations/clarification clicks.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 reports higher acceptance when evidence, recommendation, and action controls are separated, and more cancellations/clarification clicks when recommendation and action are combined. S6 warns against placing execution directly beside an AI recommendation and recommends separated panels.

claimJudgments.claimText: Make it difficult to confuse generated summaries/recommendations with final commitments by ensuring drafts remain advisory and clearly distinct from approved actions.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 says generated internal drafts are advisory and asks the product to make it impossible to confuse generated summaries with final commitments. S2 supports distinguishing draft analysis/recommendations from approved actions via labels.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation directly answers which Briefing Genie actions should be automated versus require human approval. It is well supported by the packet: preparation tasks such as drafts, summaries, and artifact links can be automated with clear generated-status labeling, while policy changes, customer commitments/external messages, and release blocking require recorded human approval. The UI/status-label implementation guidance is also supported by the incident, analytics, and interface notes.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
